# Project Charta
## Context and Scope
<!-- This project focuses on the analysis of meteorite landings worldwide. Meteorites are classified based on their composition and recorded with 
attributes such as mass, location, and year of discovery.
The main goal is to explore patterns in meteorite distribution, size, and classification. The project aims to answer questions such as where 
meteorites most frequently land, how their mass varies, and which types are most common.
The expected benefit is to provide an accessible and visually intuitive understanding of meteorite data for educational and exploratory purposes.
The visualization product will be published as an online data story using a Quarto-based website hosted on GitHub Pages. -->

This project develops the "Meteorite Market & Discovery Dashboard". Beyond simple mapping, it correlates scientific NASA landing data with historical market values and geographic environmental factors. The goal is to provide a transparent valuation tool for collectors to prevent fraud and an "Opportunity Map" for meteorite hunters by identifying "Discovery Gaps" based on soil and climate data.

## Project Objectives and Success Criteria
Objective:
Enable users to understand global patterns of meteorite landings while providing a data-driven valuation framework and identifying unexplored search zones.

Success Criteria:
- Users can identify high-value "Discovery Gaps" by overlaying NASA data with aridity and land-cover layers.
- The system successfully calculates an Estimated Market Value (EMV) based on a rule-based valuation engine.
- Users can distinguish between high-market-value classes (e.g., Pallasites) and common finds.

Quantitative criteria:
- At least 4 clear visualizations covering location, mass, class, and time.
- Data includes more than 100 observations and at least 6 features.

Out of Scope:
- No real-time data updates.
- No advanced predictive modeling or machine learning.

## Stakeholder Analysis
Stakeholders include:

- Project team members: responsible for data analysis and visualization.
- Lecturers: evaluate the project and provide feedback.
- Students and general public: end users of the visualization.

The project team develops the product, lecturers assess its quality, and users benefit from understanding the data.

## User Analysis

<!-- PERSONA 1
Name: Max
Age: 22
Occupation: Student

Domain expertise: Low to medium  
Data literacy: Basic  
Device: Laptop  
Usage: Occasional  

User Tasks:
- Understand basic patterns in data
- Learn about meteorites

Pains:
- Data is hard to interpret in raw form
- Lack of clear visual explanations

Gains:
- Easy-to-understand visualizations
- Clear insights without technical knowledge

PERSONA 2
Name: Anna
Age: 35
Occupation: Journalist

Domain expertise: Medium  
Data literacy: Medium  
Device: Laptop / Tablet  
Usage: Regular  

User Tasks:
- Find interesting data stories
- Use insights for reporting

Pains:
- Time-consuming data exploration
- Lack of summarized insights

Gains:
- Quick access to patterns and trends
- Visually appealing charts for storytelling -->

Persona 1: The Amateur Investor (Kevin, 28)

User Tasks: Wants to verify if a meteorite price on eBay is fair.

Pains: Lack of market transparency; fear of scams/fakes.

Gains: A data-driven "Fair Value" benchmark based on mass and classification.


Persona 2: The Field Hunter (Sarah, 35)

User Tasks: Planning an expedition to find unrecorded meteorites.

Pains: NASA data only shows what was found, not what is still there.

Gains: A heatmap showing high-probability search zones (e.g., arid deserts with low vegetation).


## Situation Assessment
Resources:
- Datasets containing meteorite landings with attributes such as mass, location, class, and year. Aridity Index and Land Cover data to calculate the "Discovery Probability".
- A self-curated reference table derived from auction houses (e.g., Heritage Auctions) to assign Euro-values.
- Team members with basic Python and data visualization knowledge.
- Tools: Python, Quarto, GitHub.

Constraints:
- Limited time until project deadlines.
- Requirement for reproducibility.

Risks:
- Data quality issues (missing values, inconsistencies)
- Technical challenges in visualization or deployment

## Visualization Concept
The visualization product is designed to translate the project objectives into an intuitive and accessible representation of meteorite data. It enables users to explore patterns in meteorite distribution, mass, and classification in a structured and visually clear way, addressing their need for simple and understandable insights.

Product form:
<!-- The chosen format is a data story presented as a web-based application using Quarto. This format allows a guided narrative, where users are introduced step by step to the most important aspects of the dataset. At the same time, it allows light exploration, combining storytelling with elements of an exploratory tool. -->

Visual encodings:
The visualization includes multiple complementary chart types:
- A world map displaying meteorite landings based on latitude and longitude to reveal spatial patterns.
- A histogram showing the distribution of meteorite mass to identify typical ranges and outliers.
- A bar chart comparing the frequency of different meteorite classes (recclass).
- A line chart illustrating how meteorite discoveries change over time.
<<<<<<< HEAD
<<<<<<< HEAD
- 
=======
>>>>>>> parent of f12a19f (Update project_charta.qmd)
=======
>>>>>>> parent of f12a19f (Update project_charta.qmd)

These visual encodings are chosen to represent key analytical dimensions such as distribution, comparison, location, and temporal trends.

Interactivity:
The visualizations include light interactivity to support user exploration without increasing complexity. This includes:
- Tooltips to display additional information when hovering over data points
- Zooming and panning functionality for the map
- Optional highlighting or filtering (e.g. by meteorite class or time range, if feasible)

This level of interactivity allows users to engage with the data while keeping the system simple and robust.

Narrative and annotation:
The visualization follows a clear storytelling structure: introduction, exploration of key variables, and summary of findings. Each visualization is accompanied by short explanatory text and annotations to guide the user and highlight key insights. This reduces cognitive load and helps users interpret the data correctly.

Target medium and integration:
The visualization product will be published as a web-based application using GitHub Pages. This ensures easy accessibility across devices such as laptops and tablets without requiring additional software installation.

Value of the design:

Cognitive and analytical value:
The selected visual encodings enhance pattern recognition and analytical understanding. The map supports spatial reasoning, the histogram reveals distributions and outliers, the bar chart enables comparison, and the line chart highlights trends over time. Interactivity further supports exploration and deeper insight.

Communicative value:
The use of simple, well-known chart types combined with a structured narrative ensures that the information is communicated clearly to a broad audience. Annotations and explanations help users with limited data literacy understand the key insights.

Experiential and aesthetic value:
A clean and minimal visual design combined with light interactivity creates an engaging user experience. The ability to explore the data (e.g. through tooltips or zooming) increases user involvement and trust in the visualization, while maintaining clarity and usability.

Justification:
The visualization concept directly supports the project objectives by enabling users to understand meteorite patterns in an intuitive and structured way. It addresses user needs by simplifying complex data, reducing cognitive effort, and providing both guided storytelling and limited interactive exploration.


## Project Plan
Divide the project into individual phases, describe them briefly and draw up a preliminary timetable, e.g. as a Gantt chart:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart.
gantt
    title Project Plan
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    
    section Project Understanding
        Project setup            :a1, 2026-03-18, 2d
        Project charta           :a2, 2026-03-19, 1d

    section Data Exploration
        Data cleaning            :a3, 2026-03-21, 5d
        Data analysis            :a4, 2026-03-26, 5d

     section Data Enrichment
        Price Index Research     :a9, 2026-03-22, 4d
        Geospatial Layer Join    :a10, 2026-03-27, 4d

    section Visualization
        Create visualizations    :a5, 2026-04-01, 10d
        Build data story         :a6, 2026-04-11, 7d

    section Finalization
        Documentation            :a7, 2026-05-01, 7d
        Presentation prep        :a8, 2026-05-20, 5d
```


## Roles and Contact Details
List the people involved in the development work here with their role titles, tasks and contact details.
Team Members:

- Baskaran Amutha (baskaamu): Github Setup and other technicallity
- Eros Palumbo (palero01): Project charta and ideas
- Nicolas Schwaiger (schwani8): Design, Layout and Support
